meta.title: X300 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Jaguar XJ X300 engine replacement costs — 3.2, 4.0, XJR supercharged, and 6.0 V12. Classic Jaguar specialist prices, reliability data, and ownership advice from 15+ vetted specialists.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar XJ X300 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned X300 engine typically costs £1,500–£10,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the 3.2 and 4.0 AJ16 sixes sit at £1,500–£4,000, while the V12 is £5,000–£10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 depending on specialist labour rates. Classic car specialists are recommended over main dealers for these engines. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar XJ X300 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 4.0‑litre naturally aspirated AJ16 (9JPFRB) is the most reliable engine in the X300 range — distributorless ignition, revised pistons, and magnesium‑alloy valve covers made it significantly more dependable than earlier AJ6 units. The 3.2 AJ16 is equally robust but less powerful. The V12 is the least reliable and most expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar XJ X300?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most common issue is ignition coil pack failure on the AJ16 six‑cylinder engines — misfires and rough running are the symptoms. On the XJR, supercharger coupler wear causes a rattling noise at idle. On the V12, cooling system leaks and ignition coil failures are the main concerns. These are classic cars, so age‑related wear across all systems is expected.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar XJ X300 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The X300 is considered the most reliable classic XJ — the AJ16 six‑cylinder engines are robust and well‑sorted, the distributorless ignition system is a significant improvement over earlier models, and the build quality is better than the XJ40. The V12 is more complex and costly to maintain. As with any classic car, condition and service history matter more than the model's reputation.

mainEntity.name: Which Jaguar XJ X300 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 6.0‑litre V12 is the one we'd advise against for anyone but serious collectors — it's expensive to maintain, complex to work on, and parts are increasingly difficult to source. A neglected V12 can quickly become a money pit. The 3.2 AJ16 is underpowered for the XJ's weight — the 4.0 is the better choice.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my X300 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Misfires, rough running, loss of power, blue smoke from the exhaust, or a rattling noise from the supercharger (XJR) are the key warning signs. On the V12, overheating or coolant loss is a serious concern. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment based on your symptoms.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar XJ X300 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's provenance and condition. Against a well‑kept £8,000 X300 with history, a £2,000‑£4,000 AJ16 recon is 25‑50% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon is 65‑130% — that crosses our 60% Jaguar threshold, so we'd advise walking away. For a V12, the economics are even less favourable unless the car is a low‑mileage collector's piece.

mainEntity.name: How long do Jaguar XJ X300 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The AJ16 six‑cylinder engines can exceed 150,000‑200,000 miles with proper servicing — they're robust units. The V12 is also capable of high mileage, but its complexity and parts scarcity make it less predictable. As with any classic car, regular maintenance is the key to longevity.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the X300, XJ40, and X308?

acceptedAnswer.text: The XJ40 (1986–1994) was the previous generation with a squared‑off design and the AJ6 engine. The X300 (1994–1997) is an evolution with revised styling, distributorless ignition, and the AJ16 engine. The X308 (1997–2003) introduced the AJ‑V8 engine and a further styling update. This page covers the X300 only — earlier and later models have different engine options and reliability profiles.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar XJ X300 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. Common X300 codes include 9HPGNB (3.2 AJ16), 9JPFRB (4.0 AJ16), 9KPDRB (4.0 supercharged XJR), and the V12 has no separate code but is identified as the 6.0‑litre 12‑cylinder. Tell us your variant and registration year and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: Jaguar XJ X300 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X300 — from the 3.2‑litre AJ16 six that delivered refined waftability, to the 4.0‑litre supercharged XJR that redefined the sporting saloon, and the 6.0‑litre V12 that marked the end of an era for Jaguar's legendary twelve‑cylinder engine. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the distributorless ignition that made this generation the most reliable classic XJ of them all. Backed by pattern data and Jaguar enthusiast club sources [PATTERN DATA].

engineDatabase.h2: Jaguar XJ X300 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the X300 — from the naturally aspirated AJ16 six‑cylinder in two displacements, to the supercharged XJR, and the legendary 6.0‑litre V12 that closed the book on Jaguar's V12 era.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: The X300 is a classic car with a well‑documented enthusiast following. The AJ16 six‑cylinder engines (both 3.2 and 4.0) are considered the most reliable XJ engines of their era — distributorless ignition, revised pistons, and magnesium‑alloy valve covers made them significantly more dependable than earlier AJ6 units. The supercharged XJR engine is also well‑regarded but more complex. The V12 is the most costly to maintain and the least reliable of the range — it was deleted in 1997 when all V12 engine production finished. All ratings are [PATTERN DATA] sourced from Jaguar enthusiast clubs and specialist forums.

overview.intro: The Jaguar XJ X300 is a full‑size luxury saloon manufactured by Jaguar Cars between 1994 and 1997. It was the first XJ produced entirely under Ford Motor Company ownership and can be considered an evolution of the outgoing XJ40 generation. The design placed emphasis on improved build quality, improved reliability, and a return to traditional Jaguar styling elements. Under the bonnet, the X300 offered Jaguar's revised AJ16 inline‑six in 3.2‑litre and 4.0‑litre forms, a supercharged 4.0‑litre version for the XJR, and the legendary 6.0‑litre V12 in the XJ12 and Daimler Double Six. A significant mechanical update was the switch to distributorless electronic engine management for both the six‑cylinder and V12 engines.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Jaguar X300 (evolution of XJ40) • Predecessor: XJ40 • Successor: X308 • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (saloon only; Daimler‑badged variants were trim‑level equivalents).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The X300 is a classic car with a dedicated enthusiast following — no verified 2025 enquiry data exists for this model [PATTERN DATA].

rows.repairCostSpecialist: Solid Choice — reliable six, entry‑level running costs

rows.typicalMileage: Best Value — the sweet spot of the range

rows.repairCostSpecialist: Performance Choice — faster, costlier to maintain

economicsRule.title: The X300 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,000–£4,000 AJ16 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 X300 sits at 25–130% of the car's value. On a sub‑£4,000 X300, a major engine failure may exceed the car's value — our 60% Jaguar threshold applies. On a well‑kept classic with provenance, the same repair is an investment, not a cost. Value the car first, then decide.

problems.title: AJ16 Ignition Coil Pack Failure
problems.affectedModels: All X300 six‑cylinder models (3.2 and 4.0, naturally aspirated and supercharged).
problems.rootCause: The distributorless ignition system uses coilpacks driven by Denso ignition modules. These coilpacks can fail with age, leading to misfires, rough running, and reduced power. The system is generally reliable, but coilpack failure is the most common electrical issue on the AJ16.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of misfire or rough running on an X300, get the ignition system diagnosed immediately — a £200–£500 coilpack replacement is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000 engine replacement that follows if ignored. This is the most common issue on the AJ16 and the easiest to prevent.

cta.label: Compare AJ16 ignition parts for your X300 →

problems.title: V12 Ignition and Cooling System Issues
problems.affectedModels: XJ12 and Daimler Double Six (6.0 V12).
problems.rootCause: The V12's distributorless ignition system uses two packs of three coils each. Coil failure is common, and the cooling system is complex and prone to leaks. The V12 is also sensitive to overheating — a single overheating event can warp the aluminium cylinder heads.
problems.recommendation: On any X300 V12, have the cooling system inspected annually and replace ignition coils at the first sign of misfire. A £500–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £5,000–£10,000 V12 rebuild that follows an overheat event. These cars are for collectors with budgets to match.

problems.title: Supercharger Coupler Wear (XJR)
problems.rootCause: The Eaton M90 supercharger has a coupler that connects the drive pulley to the rotors. This coupler can wear over time, leading to a rattling noise from the supercharger at idle. If ignored, the coupler can fail completely, causing supercharger damage.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a rattling noise from the supercharger at idle on an XJR, get the coupler inspected immediately — a £500–£1,200 coupler replacement is far cheaper than the £2,000–£4,000 supercharger rebuild that follows if ignored. This is the XJR's most common supercharger issue and is easily preventable.

replacementCosts.h2: X300 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are X300 saloon only. The XJR is the supercharged 4.0‑litre performance variant. The XJ12 and Daimler Double Six share the 6.0‑litre V12. Daimler‑badged variants (Daimler Six, Daimler Double Six) were trim‑level equivalents of the Sovereign and XJ12 respectively. The X300 was also available as the XJ Sport, Sovereign, and Vanden Plas models.
